key_points: Laser-activated irrigation (LAI) uses pulsed erbium lasers (Er:YAG, Er;Cr:YSGG) to generate cavitation bubbles in root canal irrigants, producing intense fluid dynamics throughout the canal system.

Cavitation is the primary mechanism of LAI: rapidly growing and imploding vapour bubbles cause high-speed fluid motion, shock waves (in free reservoirs), and secondary cavitation, leading to irrigant movement and biofilm disruption.

Key parameters influencing LAI efficacy include: laser wavelength (erbium wavelengths have absorption coefficients of 7000–13,000 cm⁻¹ in water), pulse energy (directly proportional to bubble size), pulse duration (inversely proportional to bubble size), and fibre tip geometry (conical tips produce larger spherical bubbles vs. ellipsoidal bubbles from flat tips).
Diode lasers (810–1064 nm, alpha <0.2 cm⁻¹) are not suited for cavitation-based LAI due to insufficient water absorption; a minimum absorption coefficient of 10 cm⁻¹ is required.

Historical trend in LAI: from intracanal fibre placement with flat tips and high pulse energies (60–80 mJ), to coronal/pulp chamber placement with conical tips and lower pulse energies (10–20 mJ for PIPS).

PIPS (photon-induced photoacoustic streaming) uses a conical tip in the pulp chamber with 50 µs pulses and 10–20 mJ; Biolase/Waterlase Er;Cr:YSGG protocol uses intracanal placement 1–5 mm from working length with 25 mJ pulses.

SWEEPS (shock wave enhanced emission photoacoustic streaming) uses double Er:YAG pulse pairs to generate shock waves even in confined canal spaces; evidence for superiority over single-pulse LAI is currently lacking.

Laboratory studies in extracted teeth suggest LAI is superior to conventional syringe irrigation for canal disinfection, and there is a trend of higher antimicrobial efficacy compared to ultrasonic activation (UAI), though significant methodological heterogeneity exists.

Clinical evidence is limited: multiple RCTs show LAI does not increase postoperative pain versus no activation or UAI; only one RCT assessed healing of apical periodontitis (Er;Cr:YSGG vs. conventional, no significant difference at 1 year).
LAI effectively resolves the apical vapour lock effect by progressively breaking down entrapped air bubbles.
Temperature increases at the apical root surface during LAI are clinically safe (max ~2.5°C under clinically relevant settings).

Er:YAG LAI can be safely used under the dental operating microscope (zero transmission of Er:YAG radiation through the microscope optics); wavelength-matched eye protection is mandatory for all personnel and patients.
LAI activation of NaOCl and EDTA may cause pronounced surface disintegration of root canal dentine, greater than sonic or ultrasonic activation.
No standardized LAI protocol exists; significant variability in parameters across studies complicates synthesis of results.

outcomes.measured: Canal disinfection (antimicrobial efficacy) vs. conventional syringe irrigation
outcomes.result: LAI superior to conventional irrigation in the majority of selected laboratory studies (extracted teeth)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Consistent trend across multiple in vitro studies, but clinical confirmation lacking

outcomes.measured: Canal disinfection (antimicrobial efficacy) vs. ultrasonic activation (UAI)
outcomes.result: Trend of higher antimicrobial efficacy with LAI vs. UAI; LAI superior in 5/8 studies in one systematic review, no difference in 3/8
outcomes.clinical_significance: Trend favoring LAI but not consistently significant across all studies

outcomes.measured: Postoperative pain after LAI vs. no activation or UAI
outcomes.result: No higher postoperative pain with LAI in multiple RCTs; some RCTs found lower pain scores with LAI systems vs. other activation methods
outcomes.clinical_significance: LAI does not increase postoperative pain risk

outcomes.measured: Healing of apical periodontitis (Er;Cr:YSGG LAI vs. conventional treatment)
outcomes.result: No significant difference in apical healing between groups at 1 year (single RCT)
outcomes.clinical_significance: Insufficient clinical evidence to confirm benefit on healing outcomes

outcomes.measured: Intracanal fluid velocity induced by LAI vs. UAI
outcomes.result: LAI (PIPS) induced fluid speeds 0.3–0.5 m/s, approximately 10 times higher than ultrasonically oscillating file; apical region speeds 1–4 m/s; significant fluid movement up to 19 mm from fibre tip
outcomes.clinical_significance: Demonstrates superior irrigant dynamics and remote cleaning capability of LAI

outcomes.measured: Temperature change at apical root surface during LAI
outcomes.result: Maximum increase of 2.5°C under clinically relevant settings for both Er:YAG and Er;Cr:YSGG
No significant difference in dentinal microhardness between PIPS-activated and non-activated irrigation solutions
outcomes.clinical_significance: PIPS activation does not adversely affect dentin hardness

outcomes.measured: Root canal dentine surface disintegration with LAI vs. sonic/ultrasonic activation
outcomes.result: LAI activation of NaOCl and EDTA led to pronounced surface disintegration, higher than sonic (Eddy) or ultrasonic activation
outcomes.clinical_significance: Potential concern for dentin integrity with LAI; requires further investigation

notes: This narrative review applies strict selection criteria to evaluate antimicrobial efficacy studies (Table 1), selecting 6 papers meeting methodological standards. The review covers physical principles, historical trends, parameter effects, PIPS, SWEEPS, and clinical/laboratory evidence. Conflict of interest: Dr. Roeland De Moor reports personal fees from Fotona (manufacturer of Er:YAG laser systems including Lightwalker). No funding declared.

population.condition: Apical periodontitis / root canal infection requiring disinfection
population.sample_type: Narrative review of laboratory and clinical studies; primary studies used extracted human teeth and clinical trial patients

superiority_claim: The authors state: 'it can be stated that canal disinfection with LAI is superior to that of conventional irrigation, and there is a clear trend of higher antimicrobial efficacy of LAI compared to ultrasonic activation.' Additionally, regarding wavelengths: 'When experimentally determining the vapour bubble size and resulting fluid movement of these three wavelengths, the Er:YAG was found to be the most effective in cavitation bubble formation and in generating fluid motions within narrow lateral canals.'
conflicts_of_interest: Maarten Meire reports nonfinancial support and Dr. Roeland De Moor reports personal fees from Fotona outside of the submitted work.
source_citation: Meire, M., & De Moor, R. J. G. (2024). Principle and antimicrobial efficacy of laser-activated irrigation: A narrative review. International Endodontic Journal, 57, 841–860. https://doi.org/10.1111/iej.14042
contraindications: Diode lasers (810–1064 nm) are not suited for cavitation-based LAI due to insufficient water absorption (alpha <0.2 cm⁻¹)
Eye protection (wavelength-matched) is mandatory for patient and all personnel during LAI
adverse_events: Potential increased apical pressure and irrigant extrusion demonstrated in laboratory models (not confirmed as clinically significant in RCTs)
Pronounced surface disintegration of root canal dentine with LAI activation of NaOCl and EDTA (greater than sonic or ultrasonic activation)

anatomical_points: 1–5 mm from working length (Er;Cr:YSGG intracanal approach)

clinical_indication: Root canal disinfection / treatment of apical periodontitis
target_tissue: dentin, root canal system (pulp chamber, main canals, lateral canals, dentinal tubules)
uncertainties: Laser parameters reported in Table 1 are from cited primary studies, not from a single standardized protocol; multiple parameter sets exist across studies.
Pulse duration for the Biolase Waterlase Er;Cr:YSGG recommended protocol (25 mJ intracanal) is not explicitly stated in the review.
The review does not provide a single definitive recommended LAI protocol; authors explicitly state guidelines are lacking.
Clinical evidence on healing outcomes is based on a single RCT (Martins et al., 2014), limiting generalizability.
The superiority claim regarding LAI vs. UAI is based on heterogeneous laboratory studies; clinical confirmation is absent.
